ontology.foodex2_description: Biscuits and cookies — cream-filled sandwich type
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for mini cream-filled biscuit; A0FKP (biscuits and cookies) used as closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this processed snack food category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is approximately 30 g (about 5 mini biscuits), providing roughly 161 kcal, 8 g fat, and 19 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Mini Cream Filled Biscuit — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mini Cream Filled Biscuit (cr_id F11080039). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Biscuits and Cream-Filled Sandwich Cookies — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry for mini cream-filled biscuit found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 (high) is estimated from the closest analogous entry: plain sweet biscuits and cream sandwich cookies, which consistently return GI values of 65–75 in peer-reviewed GI testing. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 19.0) / 100 = 13.3. Serving size used: 30 g (19.0 g available carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (38.15 g/100 g), high saturated fat (~12 g/100 g), and moderate sodium (262 mg/100 g) with zero dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid mini cream-filled biscuits; the combination of high sugar (38.15 g/100 g), high saturated fat, and zero fibre promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention, weight gain, and arterial stiffness, all of which worsen blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar (38.15 g/100 g), high refined carbohydrate (63.11 g/100 g), zero dietary fibre, and high GI (70)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mini cream-filled biscuits are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high GI (70), very high sugar content (38.15 g/100 g), and complete absence of dietary fibre cause rapid and severe postprandial blood glucose spikes with no safe portion size for glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12 g/100 g), trans fat (~1.5 g/100 g from hydrogenated shortening), and high sugar (38.15 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mini cream-filled biscuits pose a high cardiovascular risk due to their trans fat content (~1.5 g/100 g), which raises LDL cholesterol and lowers HDL cholesterol, combined with high saturated fat and sugar that promote dyslipidaemia, endothelial dysfunction, and atherosclerosis progression.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mini cream-filled biscuits are bite-sized ultra-processed sandwich cookies consisting of two thin, sweet baked biscuit wafers enclosing a thick, sweet cream filling. They are manufactured using refined wheat flour, sugar, vegetable shortening (often partially hydrogenated palm oil), and flavouring agents. The biscuits are crisp and lightly sweet, while the cream filling is dense, very sweet, and fatty. They are widely sold in Singapore school canteens, convenience stores, and supermarkets as an affordable snack food, typically in small individual packs of 25–35 g. Nutritionally, mini cream-filled biscuits are energy-dense at 537 kcal/100 g, with high fat (26.7 g/100 g, of which approximately 12 g is saturated fat and ~1.5 g is trans fat from hydrogenated shortening), very high sugar (38.15 g/100 g), and high refined carbohydrate (63.11 g/100 g). Dietary fibre is absent (0.0 g/100 g), and sodium is moderate at 262 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is high (GI ~70), producing a glycemic load of approximately 13.3 per 30 g serving. These biscuits are classified as ultra-processed and carry high concern ratings for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to their trans fat, saturated fat, high sugar, and zero fibre profile. They are not suitable for any clinical dietary management context and should be consumed only occasionally by healthy individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mini cream biscuits?
Are sandwich cookies high in sugar?
Is cream biscuit good for hypertension?
What is the GI of cream-filled cookies?
How much fat is in mini cream biscuits?
Are cream biscuits suitable for heart patients?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080039) at confidence 0.95. GI is estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database (sweet biscuits and cream sandwich cookies, GI 65–75), yielding medium confidence for GI specifically; overall record confidence is high given exact HPB source match for all macros and secondary nutrients.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an ultra-processed biscuit with no fibre-containing ingredients by formulation, confirmed by HPB source. calcium_mg and iron_mg are confirmed zero per the HPB database entry, not missing data. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this product are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F11080039 as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g of 1.5 g/100 g is estimated from product category knowledge (hydrogenated shortening in cream-filled biscuits); this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB source and is flagged as an estimate. saturated_fat_g of 12.0 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry for cream sandwich cookies (USDA SR Legacy item 18167) and is consistent with the total fat of 26.7 g/100 g. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on sweet biscuits and cream sandwich cookies; no exact entry for mini cream-filled biscuit was found. This food is classified as western/global and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under the G6 multilingual rule.
